form_analysis.form_header_text: FY2027 Appropriations Request - Office of Rep. Bill Keating (MA-09)
form_analysis.form_instructions_text: Please fill out one form per request. To the extent possible, please provide detailed information regarding the location of your request in the subcommittee bill or report, down to the activity, account, or country level (if applicable). Should you have any questions, please contact Ryan Maddock at Ryan.Maddock@mail.house.gov or (202) 225-2511

fields.label: Requesting Entity
fields.help_text: Name of the organization or entity requesting funding or language support.

fields.label: Request Type
fields.help_text: Select the type of appropriation request being submitted.
fields.rationale: The pre-computed reference values explicitly categorize this as a programmatic funding request for the Science Mission Directorate.

fields.label: Subcommittee
fields.help_text: The relevant House Appropriations Subcommittee for this request.
options: Labor, Health, and Human Services (Including Education)
fields.rationale: The reference context identifies the Commerce, Justice, Science, and Related Agencies Subcommittee as the appropriate jurisdiction for NASA Science.

fields.label: If you chose "Defense" - Which account?
fields.rationale: This is a non-defense programmatic request for NASA Science under the CJS subcommittee.

fields.label: Description of Program Request
fields.help_text: Provide a brief overview of the program being requested.
fields.draft_value: This request provides $9 billion for NASA's Science Mission Directorate (SMD). SMD conducts vital research across Earth science, planetary science, astrophysics, and heliophysics, supporting MA-09 institutions like Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution, which received over $24 million in recent NASA funding for critical ocean and planetary research.
fields.rationale: Provides a concise description of SMD and highlights local impact in MA-09, specifically citing the $24.2M in grants to Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution found in the spending context.

fields.label: Program Funding Amount
fields.help_text: Specify the dollar amount requested for the program.
fields.rationale: Matches the requested funding amount for NASA Science Mission Directorate provided in the asks_config.

fields.label: For Bill or Report Language Requests - What is the Agency?
fields.rationale: The request context indicates this is a programmatic funding request, not a bill or report language request.

fields.label: For Bill or Report Language Requests - What is the Office?
fields.rationale: The request context indicates this is a programmatic funding request, not a bill or report language request.

fields.label: For Bill or Report Language Requests - What is the Program Title?
fields.rationale: The request context indicates this is a programmatic funding request, not a bill or report language request.

fields.label: Has Congressman Keating supported this line item in the past?
fields.rationale: Historical voting or support records for this specific member on this line item are not provided in the context; requiring submitter knowledge.

fields.label: Has this line item been submitted before?
fields.rationale: The context does not specify if this exact request has been submitted by this office in previous fiscal years.

fields.label: Justification for Language
fields.help_text: Provide detailed reasoning for the requested bill or report language.
fields.rationale: The request is categorized as a programmatic funding request ($9 billion) and the provided context specifies that no report or bill language is requested (N/A). As per instructions for mutually exclusive sections, this field is non-applicable.

fields.label: Nexus to interests of the 9th Congressional District of Massachusetts
fields.help_text: Explain how this request directly benefits or impacts the residents and interests of MA-09.
fields.draft_value:
* NASA Science invested over $20.9M in MA-09 institutions and businesses between FY2023 and FY2025, supporting high-skill research jobs.
* Sustains critical research at Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution (WHOI), including $6M for climate modeling and $1.6M to investigate life on ice-covered ocean worlds.
* Supports local specialized manufacturers including McLane Research Laboratories ($320k) and Remote Sensing Solutions ($134k) for mission-critical instrumentation.
* Strengthens the district's blue economy and STEM pipeline by leveraging maritime expertise for planetary exploration and Earth science.
fields.rationale: Uses specific local spending data ($20.9M total) and cites top grant recipient Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution along with local contract recipients McLane Research Laboratories and Remote Sensing Solutions as found in the NASA spending context.

top_grants.description: A major difficulty in simulating Earth S climate system is that there are interactions across scales  so that the large time and space scales can be sensitive to processes on small scales. As the computational resolution of global Ocean models has improved  scientists have begun to suspect that kilometer-scale eddies and fronts  called  submesoscale  variability  have a net effect on ocean-atmosphere heat exchange that is larger than the heating from the greenhouse effect (su et al. 2018). State-of-the-art computer models agree in predicting that these eddies have important long-term effects on the upper-ocean  but their predictions are sensitive to relatively small details in model physics and implementation. The resolution and detail of these simulations has surpassed our ability to  ground truth  them with spaceborne or in situ sensors. There is thus a pressing need for a comprehensive benchmark data set on these submesoscale motions to address this important source of uncertainty in simulating the global Ocean. This is a proposal to test the hypothesis that submesoscale Ocean Dynamics make important contributions to vertical exchange of climate and biological variables in the upper Ocean. This will require coordinated application of newly-developed in situ and remote sensing techniques  and it will provide an unprecedented view of the physics of submesoscale eddies and fronts and their effects on vertical transport in the upper Ocean. The sub-mesoscale Ocean Dynamics Experiment (S-MODE) will use measurements from a novel combination of platforms and instruments)  along with data analysis and modeling  to test the hypothesis.
top_grants.generated_award_summary: Measuring small ocean eddies to reduce uncertainty in climate modeling.

top_grants.description: e014042-science and technology for ocean worlds: ice-covered chemosynthetic ecosystems.
top_grants.generated_award_summary: Investigating life in ice-covered ocean worlds.

top_grants.description: Here  we propose to test the hypotheses of planetesimal evaporation and core formation to explain the ~0.2  v isotopic difference between the bse and chondrites  and thereby place constraints on the accretionary and differentiation history of Earth and other differentiated bodies like Mars and 4 Vesta. The first proposed task investigates the isotopic consequences of v evaporation from objects ranging from refractory inclusions in chondrites to planetary magma oceans. We will carry out stepwise evaporation experiments whereby synthetic cais and chondritic melts will be subject to partial evaporation  and the isotope fractionation of the similarly volatile elements v and sr will be measured. In the second task we will analyze the v and sr isotope compositions of major achondrite classes  including angrites  heds  ureilites  brachinites  and aubrites. Lastly  the third task will involve new experimental investigations of v isotope fractionation between liquid metal and silicate melt at high pressure-temperature and under various conditions (p  t  fo2  composition). Taken together  these data will enable determining whether the heavy v isotope feature of the bse is most likely inherited from accretion of material akin to achondrite parent bodies  or from high pressure-temperature differentiation processes that only occurred on terrestrial planets. This will allow us to address fundamental questions such as: 1) which process(ES) relevant to planetary formation and differentiation account for the heavy v isotope composition of bse and bsm? 2) what is the genetic relationship between chondrites  achondrites and the terrestrial planets  and what are the implications for the starting composition of the Earth? 3) under which conditions (E.g.  Pressure  oxygen fugacity) did planetary differentiation proceed in the early solar system  including on Earth?
top_grants.generated_award_summary: Determining early Earth composition via high-pressure isotope studies.

top_grants.description: Aqueous alteration of a ci chondrite simulant relevant to Enceladus and other primitive solar system objects: constraints.from laboratory experiments
top_grants.generated_award_summary: Investigating water's effect on Enceladus-like materials via lab experiments.
